title: NCA-GENL · Trustworthy AI
description: Prompt injection, hallucination, bias, PII, model cards, consent, human-in-the-loop (exam domain 5, 10%).

cards.front: What is the primary risk of prompt injection?
cards.back: Untrusted content (in user input, retrieved docs, or tool outputs) overrides the system instructions and causes the model to perform unintended actions or leak data. Architectural mitigations — separate instructions from data, output validators, least-privilege tools, never trusting the model with high-stakes actions — matter more than prompt-level patches, because no prompt is fully proof against injection.
cards.quizAnswer: Untrusted content overrides the system instructions, making the model take unintended actions or leak data.
distractors: It raises GPU bills because injected text makes the model generate far longer responses.
It slows training convergence by corrupting the gradients during the fine-tuning loop.
It reduces the model's perplexity, making the evaluation metrics look artificially good.
It causes the model to refuse every request, creating a denial-of-service condition.
It leaks the model's weights to the attacker by encoding them in the output tokens.
It increases inference latency because the injected prompt must be re-tokenized twice.

cards.front: Which is a recommended approach to reduce LLM hallucinations in production?
cards.back: Ground responses with retrieval (RAG), require citations, lower temperature, and add a faithfulness check. Hallucinations come from generation untethered from a knowledge source; grounding + citations + verification drastically reduces them, while raising temperature does the opposite.
cards.quizAnswer: Ground answers with retrieval (RAG), require citations, lower temperature, and add a faithfulness check.
distractors: Increase the temperature to 1.5 so the model explores more answers and self-corrects.
Disable output validation so the model is free to produce its most confident answer.
Remove the system prompt so the model relies entirely on its pretrained knowledge.
Fine-tune the model on more web text so it memorizes a wider range of facts.
Raise the top-p value to widen the candidate set and dilute the incorrect tokens.
Shorten the context window so the model has fewer chances to drift off topic.

cards.front: Why is bias auditing important for an LLM-powered application?
cards.back: Compliance frameworks and ethical-deployment requirements demand that models not produce systematically harmful or unequal outcomes across protected groups. Bias audits combine subgroup metrics, counterfactual tests, and red-teaming; documenting findings (model card, datasheet) is part of NVIDIA's Trustworthy AI pillar and most enterprise governance frameworks.
cards.quizAnswer: Compliance and ethics require that the model not produce systematically harmful or unequal outcomes across protected groups.
distractors: It speeds up training by removing the biased examples that slow gradient convergence.
It reduces GPU memory because de-biased models have fewer parameters to store.
It is required by CUDA, which refuses to run models that fail a fairness check.
It improves the model's perplexity by smoothing the output probability distribution.
It lowers inference latency because unbiased models produce shorter responses.
It is only needed for research papers, not for production-deployed applications.

cards.front: What is PII and why must it be handled carefully in LLM workflows?
cards.back: Personally Identifiable Information; it can identify a real person and is regulated by laws (GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA), so it must be masked, minimized, or removed from training data, prompts, logs, and outputs. Standard practice: scrub PII at ingestion (regex + classifier), redact in logs, and avoid sending it to third-party APIs without contractual coverage. Outputs can leak training-data PII — scan and block.
cards.quizAnswer: Personally Identifiable Information — regulated data that must be masked or removed from training, prompts, logs, and outputs.
distractors: Pretrained Inference Index; a lookup table that can be safely ignored during deployment.
Public Internet Information; data scraped from the web that requires no special protection.
Periodic Index of Iterations; a training-loop counter that is harmless to expose.
Parallel Inference Instances; replicas of the model that must be load-balanced carefully.
Private Inference Input; the raw logits that should be encrypted before leaving the GPU.
Protected Internal Identifier; a model-version string with no privacy implications.

cards.front: What is the best definition of a model card?
cards.back: A short structured document describing a model's intended use, training data, performance across groups, limitations, and ethical considerations. Model cards (Mitchell et al., 2019) are standard for transparent AI release — they communicate to downstream users what the model should and should not be used for.
cards.quizAnswer: A structured document describing a model's intended use, training data, group performance, limitations, and ethics.
distractors: A purchase invoice for the GPU on which the model was trained and deployed.
A type of cooling card installed alongside the GPU to manage its thermal load.
The serial number of the model file, used to track it across deployments.
A benchmark scoreboard ranking the model against competitors on a single metric.
A configuration file listing the model's hyperparameters for reproducible training.
A license key that authorizes the model to run on a particular GPU cluster.

cards.front: Why is user consent relevant when collecting data for LLM applications?
cards.back: Many jurisdictions require informed, specific, and revocable consent for collecting and processing personal data; violating this exposes the organization to regulatory and reputational risk. Privacy by design starts at consent, with clear retention policies and a deletion/right-to-be-forgotten path. 'Public' does not mean 'unrestricted use' — copyright and contextual integrity still apply.
cards.quizAnswer: Many laws require informed, specific, revocable consent to process personal data; violating it brings legal and reputational risk.
distractors: It is not relevant as long as the data was found somewhere on the public internet.
It only matters in research papers and academic settings, not in production systems.
It is fully replaced by encrypting the data at rest, which removes any consent obligation.
Consent is required only for the model's outputs, not for the data used to train it.
It applies only to data about employees, not to data collected from end users.
Anonymizing the data once removes the need for consent even if it can be re-identified.

cards.front: Which is a sign that an LLM output may need a human-in-the-loop review?
cards.back: The decision is high-stakes (financial, medical, legal), the model expresses low confidence, or guardrails flag potential bias or a policy violation. High-stakes domains require human oversight regardless of model confidence; confidence flags plus content classifiers route only the risky subset to humans, balancing throughput and safety.
cards.quizAnswer: When the decision is high-stakes, the model's confidence is low, or guardrails flag bias or a policy violation.
distractors: Whenever the output is short, since brief responses are more likely to be wrong.
Whenever the model used FP16 precision, because reduced precision lowers reliability.
Whenever the prompt was written in English rather than the user's native language.
Whenever the response takes longer than one second to generate on the GPU.
Whenever the model retrieved more than five documents to ground its answer.
Whenever the temperature was set above zero, since any sampling adds risk.
